review_summary: The segment is well suited for concept questions comparing hand-crafted features, end-to-end learning, and the need for image-aware operators.

question_bank.question: In a classical feature-engineering pipeline, what is the role of the feature extractor?
choices: Convert raw pixels into a human-designed representation before training the classifier.
Randomly relabel the images so the classifier becomes more robust.
Remove the need for a classifier by directly outputting the final label.
Guarantee that the model is trained end-to-end by gradient descent.
question_bank.explanation: Classical pipelines separate representation design from classifier learning. Human-designed features try to expose useful structure before the learned classifier sees the data.

question_bank.question: What information does a color histogram keep, and what does it discard?
choices: It keeps color distribution but discards where those colors occur spatially.
It keeps object shapes but discards all color values.
It keeps exact pixel locations and discards category labels.
It keeps only diagonal edges and discards color counts.
question_bank.explanation: A color histogram counts how much of each color appears. Because it does not preserve spatial layout, two images with the same colors in different places can look identical to this representation.

question_bank.question: What is the key difference between end-to-end learning and a hand-crafted feature pipeline?
choices: End-to-end learning optimizes the whole pixels-to-scores mapping from data.
End-to-end learning freezes the feature extractor and trains only the last classifier.
End-to-end learning replaces gradient descent with manually written image rules.
End-to-end learning ignores raw pixels and uses only class names.
question_bank.explanation: End-to-end learning lets gradient descent tune all parts of the mapping, including the representation. This can discover features better matched to the task than fixed hand-coded descriptors.
